title: Analysis, Evaluation, and Full Decision Reports
nceaFocus: AS92028: Bringing all achievement levels together — from describing the financial situation through to Excellence-level evaluation with putake-grounded response strategies
objectives: Write a complete financial decision-making report at the Merit to Excellence level
Evaluate the extent to which a decision considers multiple stakeholder perspectives
Demonstrate how an organisation can respond to consequences in a way that upholds putake

vocabulary.definition: Whether an organisation has enough income to cover its costs and continue operating over time. A financially viable organisation is not necessarily profitable — it just has enough money to keep doing what it exists to do.
A backup plan or financial reserve set aside to deal with unexpected events. Good financial decision-making includes contingency planning.

Money provided by a government body, trust, or foundation that does not need to be repaid. In New Zealand, common grant sources include Creative New Zealand, Lotteries Commission, Foundation North, NZ Community Trust, and local councils.

The process of making regular repayments on a loan, including both the principal (the original amount borrowed) and the interest. Debt servicing costs reduce the amount of money available for other expenses.

activities.title: How to Write a Complete Financial Decision-Making Report
activities.content:
In your AS92028 assessment, you will need to write a financial decision-making report about a real or fictional NZ organisation. This is not an essay in the traditional sense — it has a specific structure that maps directly to the achievement criteria. Understanding that structure and following it precisely is the most efficient way to perform well.

Here is the structure you should use.

Part 1: The Situation (Achieved)
Start by introducing the organisation. Name it, describe its putake, and describe the financial need, issue, or opportunity it is facing. Be specific. Use dollar figures where you have them. Do not write a paragraph of general background — get straight to the point. What is the organisation, why does it exist, and what financial situation is it facing right now?

Example of a strong Part 1 opening: 'The Ōtāhuhu Community Boxing Club is a not-for-profit organisation whose putake is to provide low-cost boxing training and youth development pathways for young people in South Auckland. The club currently faces a financial issue: annual income of $22,400 is no longer covering annual costs of $25,100, resulting in a projected annual deficit of $2,700 for the second year running. Without action, the club's reserves of $8,900 will be exhausted within approximately three years.'

That is one paragraph. It is specific. It has dollar figures. It names the putake. It identifies the type of financial situation. That is everything Part 1 needs.

Part 2: The Options and Financial Tool (Achieved)
Present at least two options and use a financial tool to compare them. Your tool must include actual dollar amounts — not estimates or ranges, but calculated figures. Label your tool clearly. Show all calculations. After presenting the tool, state clearly which option you recommend and why, connecting the recommendation to both the financial evidence and the organisation's putake.

Part 3: Stakeholder Perspectives (Merit)
Identify at least two stakeholders. For each one, explain: what they want from this decision, what they are worried about, and why it matters in the context of the organisation's putake. Use the framework: [Stakeholder] would [position] this decision because [specific reason]. Their main concern is [specific worry or desire] because [why it matters to them].

Part 4: Consequences (Merit)
Explain at least two consequences — one financial and one non-financial. Be specific. Use dollar figures where possible. Show that the consequence follows logically from the decision.

Part 5: Excellence Analysis
Do two things:
First, analyse the extent to which your recommended decision considers the perspectives of each stakeholder. Does it fully address their concerns, partially address them, or fail to address them? Explain your judgement.
Second, identify the most significant negative consequence your decision might produce, and analyse how the organisation could respond to that consequence in a way that upholds their putake.

Part 6: Conclusion
End with one strong paragraph. Summarise why your recommended option is the best choice — not because it is the cheapest or the most profitable, but because it best serves the organisation's putake while remaining financially sustainable.

This six-part structure, followed precisely, gives you everything you need for Excellence. It is not complicated. It is just clear, specific, and grounded in putake at every stage.

activities.title: Common Mistakes in AS92028 Responses — and How to Fix Them
activities.content:
Before you write your final project and sit your assessment, let's look at the most common mistakes students make in AS92028 and exactly how to fix them. These patterns come up again and again, and knowing them in advance gives you a significant advantage.

Mistake 1: Describing putake once and then never mentioning it again.
This is the most common mistake at the Achieved level. Students define putake correctly in their introduction but then completely ignore it when making their recommendation and analysing stakeholders. Putake needs to appear throughout your response — in the recommendation, in the stakeholder analysis, and especially in the Excellence section. Putake is not a definition to include once. It is a lens to apply throughout.

Mistake 2: Using a financial tool without numbers.
Students will write 'a budget shows this option is better' without actually constructing a budget with figures. A financial tool must contain actual calculated dollar amounts. If your budget does not have income and expense lines with numbers, it is not a valid financial tool for this standard. Every line needs a figure. Every total needs to be calculated. Every comparison needs to show the difference.

Mistake 3: Listing consequences without explaining them.
At the Merit level, consequence means more than 'this will happen.' You need to explain what will happen, why it happens, and how significant it is. 'The club will have less money' is not a consequence explanation. 'The $790 interest cost reduces the club's annual surplus from $2,300 to $1,510, which means the free junior programme can cover 8 fewer participants than planned' is a consequence explanation.

Mistake 4: Treating Excellence as just more Merit.
Excellence is not a longer Merit response. It is a different kind of response. Merit explains. Excellence evaluates. The key move at Excellence is the judgement — how well, to what extent, what should they do in response. If you find yourself writing more explanation without making a judgement, you are still in Merit territory. The moment you write 'to the extent that...' or 'this only partially addresses...' or 'the organisation should respond by...' — you are in Excellence territory.

Mistake 5: Recommending the cheapest option without connecting to putake.
Many students default to recommending whichever option costs the least. This is not necessarily wrong, but it needs to be justified in terms of putake, not just price. Sometimes the more expensive option better serves the organisation's reason for existing. The correct recommendation is always the one that best balances financial sustainability with putake.

Mistake 6: Writing generic stakeholder perspectives.
'The employees are affected' is not a stakeholder perspective. Neither is 'the community benefits.' You need the specific perspective of a specific stakeholder in the context of this specific decision. Use names, use figures, use the organisation's actual situation. A generic perspective earns generic marks.

Fix all six of these mistakes in your project and assessment, and you are in a very strong position for Merit and Excellence. None of them require extra knowledge. They all just require more precision and more consistent application of putake.

activities.title: Merit to Excellence Scenario — The Christchurch Rowing Club Boatshed
activities.setup:
The Christchurch Rowing Club has 210 members across junior, senior, and masters divisions. Their putake is to develop competitive rowing in Canterbury and create a lifelong sporting community for people of all ages and abilities. Annual income: member fees $31,500, fundraising events $8,200, canteen $4,600, sponsorships $5,800. Total: $50,100. Annual costs: boatshed lease $9,600, equipment maintenance $6,400, coaching staff $18,000, insurance $2,800, administration $1,900, regatta transport $3,200. Total costs: $41,900. Annual surplus: $8,200. Reserves: $22,400.

The boatshed lease expires in 14 months. The landowner has offered two renewal options:

Option A: Standard 5-year lease at $11,200 per year (increase of $1,600 per year from current $9,600).
Option B: 10-year lease at $10,400 per year (increase of $800 per year from current $9,600) with a one-off lease signing fee of $4,500.

If the club does not renew either lease, they would need to find a new boatshed location. Relocating would cost an estimated $35,000 to $50,000 and could take 6 to 12 months, during which time the club would be unable to operate fully.
activities.challenge:
1. Identify the type of financial situation and explain your reasoning.
2. Build a price comparison chart comparing the full cost of Option A and Option B over 10 years, including all additional costs above current lease payments.
3. Identify two stakeholders and explain each one's perspective on the lease decision.
4. Explain one financial and one non-financial consequence of choosing Option B.
5. Analyse the extent to which Option B considers the perspectives of the stakeholders you have identified.
6. The club's reserves are $22,400. Analyse how the club could respond to the one-off signing fee of $4,500 under Option B in a way that upholds their putake, and recommend which lease option the club should choose.
hints: For the price comparison chart, calculate the total additional lease cost above current payments over 10 years for each option. Remember Option B has the one-off signing fee of $4,500 in addition to the annual increase.
For question 5, think about whether Option B fully or only partially addresses what each stakeholder wants. Use the language of extent: 'strongly,' 'partially,' or 'does not address.'
activities.solution:
Question 1: This is a financial need. While the club is currently financially healthy, the boatshed lease expiry in 14 months creates an unavoidable decision. Without a renewed lease, the club cannot operate from its current location. The cost and disruption of relocation ($35,000 to $50,000, 6 to 12 months of disrupted operations) means inaction would threaten the club's ability to function. This is not an opportunity or an issue — it is a situation the club must respond to or face losing its operational base.

Question 2: Price comparison chart

Current annual lease: $9,600

Option A — 5-year lease at $11,200 per year:
Additional annual cost vs current: $11,200 - $9,600 = $1,600
Total additional cost over 10 years (two 5-year terms at same rate): $1,600 x 10 = $16,000
No signing fee.
Total additional cost over 10 years: $16,000

Option B — 10-year lease at $10,400 per year:
Additional annual cost vs current: $10,400 - $9,600 = $800
Total additional annual cost over 10 years: $800 x 10 = $8,000
One-off signing fee: $4,500
Total additional cost over 10 years: $12,500

Saving under Option B vs Option A over 10 years: $16,000 - $12,500 = $3,500
Option B costs $3,500 less over 10 years despite the upfront signing fee.

Question 3: Stakeholder perspectives

Stakeholder 1 — Junior members' families: These families want long-term certainty for their children's rowing development. A 10-year lease provides security that the club will operate from the same location throughout their children's junior career — from age 12 through to 18 and beyond. Their main concern is continuity: switching locations mid-programme is disruptive for training schedules, transport logistics, and the social bonds children form at a specific club site. Option B strongly addresses their perspective by locking in the club's location for a decade.

Stakeholder 2 — The club committee: The committee wants financial sustainability and certainty. They are responsible for ensuring the club can meet its obligations to all 210 members. Their concern is twofold: the $4,500 signing fee is a significant one-off cost from reserves, and a 10-year commitment is a long time to lock in if the club's circumstances change. However, the $3,500 saving over 10 years and the certainty of not renegotiating in five years likely outweigh these concerns.

Question 4: Consequences of Option B

Financial consequence: The $4,500 signing fee reduces the club's reserves from $22,400 to $17,900. While still a healthy reserve level for a club with annual income of $50,100, it reduces the financial buffer available for unexpected costs. If a significant piece of equipment fails (boat hull repairs can cost $4,000 to $8,000), the club is slightly less able to absorb the cost without affecting its operating surplus.

Non-financial consequence: A 10-year lease gives the club the confidence to invest in long-term development. With location security, the committee can pursue larger capital grants from organisations like Sport Canterbury or the Lottery Grants Board. Grant bodies prefer to fund organisations with stable operations — a 10-year lease demonstrates this stability and makes the club a more attractive funding partner, potentially opening new income streams over the decade.

Question 5: Extent to which Option B considers stakeholder perspectives

Option B strongly addresses the perspectives of junior families by providing a decade of locational security — their children can progress through the full junior programme without disruption. This is the most direct benefit of the longer lease term.

Option B partially addresses the club committee's financial concerns. The $3,500 total saving over 10 years is real, but the $4,500 signing fee creates short-term cash flow pressure. The committee's concern about being locked in for 10 years is not fully addressed — if the club's membership falls significantly or circumstances change, the club has less flexibility under a 10-year term.

Overall, Option B is a strong but not perfect fit for all stakeholder perspectives.

Question 6: Responding to the signing fee in a way that upholds putake

The $4,500 signing fee is manageable. After payment, reserves sit at $17,900 — well above the prudent minimum of three months' costs ($41,900 ÷ 12 x 3 = $10,475). The payment does not threaten the club's ability to serve its members.

To uphold putake — developing competitive rowing and creating a lifelong sporting community — the club should treat the $4,500 not as a cost to minimise but as an investment in stability. To replenish reserves, the club could launch a 'Secure Our Home' one-off fundraising campaign targeting alumni members and local businesses, aiming to raise $4,000 to $5,000 within 12 months. This approach upholds putake by involving the club community in securing the club's future — turning a financial decision into a community action that strengthens the bonds the club exists to build.

Recommendation: The club should choose Option B. Over 10 years it is $3,500 cheaper than Option A, it provides the locational security that junior members' families and the club's long-term development require, and the $4,500 signing fee is comfortably covered by reserves. The signing fee should be replenished through a community fundraising campaign that itself upholds the club's putake by engaging members in shared ownership of the club's future. This decision is not just financially sound — it is the option that best serves the reason the club exists.

questions.question: Over 10 years, Option A costs an additional $16,000 above current lease payments and Option B costs an additional $12,500 (including the $4,500 signing fee). What is the correct saving under Option B?
questions.explanation: $16,000 - $12,500 = $3,500. Option B is $3,500 cheaper than Option A over 10 years. The $4,500 is the signing fee for Option B, not the saving. The $800 and $1,600 figures are the annual additional costs for Options B and A respectively — not the 10-year comparison.

questions.question: Which of the following best demonstrates Excellence-level analysis of how the club could respond to the $4,500 signing fee in a way that upholds putake?
options: The club should delay paying the signing fee as long as possible.
The club should raise membership fees by $21.43 per member to recover the $4,500.

The club should pay the fee from reserves and launch a community fundraising campaign involving members and alumni to replenish reserves, turning a financial decision into a community-building action that upholds the club's putake of creating a lifelong sporting community.
The signing fee is too high and the club should negotiate it down before committing to Option B.
questions.explanation: Option C is the Excellence-level response. It proposes a specific, practical response (pay from reserves, run a fundraising campaign), connects it to putake (community-building, lifelong sporting community), and explains why this approach is values-grounded rather than just financially efficient. Option B would solve the financial problem but raises fees across all members for a cost that could be spread differently, and does not consider whether this aligns with the club's putake.

questions.question: What is the minimum prudent reserve level for the Christchurch Rowing Club, calculated as three months of annual operating costs?
questions.explanation: Three months of operating costs = $41,900 ÷ 12 x 3 = $10,475. After paying the $4,500 signing fee, the club's reserves would sit at $17,900 — well above this minimum. This calculation reassures us that paying the signing fee from reserves does not put the club in a financially vulnerable position.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most significant difference between a Merit and an Excellence response in AS92028?
options: Excellence responses are longer than Merit responses
Excellence responses use more financial tools than Merit responses

Excellence responses make evaluative judgements about the extent to which decisions consider stakeholder perspectives and propose putake-grounded responses to consequences, rather than just explaining what will happen
Excellence responses discuss more stakeholders than Merit responses
questions.explanation: The core difference between Merit and Excellence is the type of thinking, not the length or quantity of content. Merit explains what will happen and why. Excellence evaluates the extent to which it happens and proposes specific, values-grounded responses. Writing a longer Merit response does not earn Excellence. The evaluative judgement and the putake-grounded response strategy are the distinctive marks of Excellence in AS92028.

activities.title: Project Progress Check
activities.prompt: Review what you have written for your module project so far. Using the six-part structure from today's slide, identify which parts you have completed and which still need work. Write one sentence for each part: Part 1 (situation and putake), Part 2 (options and financial tool), Part 3 (stakeholder perspectives), Part 4 (consequences), Part 5 (Excellence analysis), Part 6 (conclusion). For any part that needs work, write one sentence describing exactly what is missing.
activities.nceaLink: This reflection maps directly to the AS92028 achievement criteria. Part 1 and Part 2 cover Achieved. Parts 3 and 4 cover Merit. Parts 5 and 6 cover Excellence. Knowing where you are in the structure helps you focus your project work in tomorrow's session.
